literal: own-form, intrinsic nature; from sva- 'one's own' + rūpa 'form'
invariant_definition: *Svarūpa* names a thing's own constitutive form, what it is in itself as opposed to what it is by adjunct (*upādhi*), relation, or accidental qualification. The term carries the load of intrinsic identity: a thing's *svarūpa* is what survives the stripping away of conditioning factors. Vedānta uses *svarūpa* in two paired registers: as the essential nature of the self (*ātma-svarūpa*) and as the essential nature of Brahman (*brahma-svarūpa*), with disputes turning on whether these are identical, structurally one but distinguishable, or related by inseparable but real difference. In epistemology *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa* contrasts with *taṭastha-lakṣaṇa*, marking the difference between defining by intrinsic constitution and defining by extrinsic mark. The term anchors most school-defining moves: whether bondage belongs to *svarūpa* or only to its conditions, whether plurality is *svābhāvika* or *aupādhika*, and what is realized when liberation occurs.

per_school.definition: *Svarūpa-lakṣaṇa* of Brahman is *sat-cit-ānanda*: not three attributes inhering in a substance, but glosses on a single non-dual essence. The *ātman*'s *svarūpa* is identical with this Brahman-*svarūpa*; bondage, agency, and finitude are *aupādhika*, not *svābhāvika*. Hence liberation is recognition of an already-constituted *svarūpa*, not its production.

Bhagavān's *svarūpa* is *jñāna-ānanda*, ontologically distinct from his *guṇas* (the auspicious qualities) and his *vibhūti* (cit-acit body). The *jīva*'s *svarūpa* is *jñāna-svarūpa* and at the same time *jñāna-āśraya*, and its relation to Bhagavān is *aprthak-siddha* — inseparable but really distinct. *Svarūpa* therefore carries the weight of Rāmānuja's whole anti-Advaitic move: real distinction is built into the constitutive natures themselves.

Each *jīva* has an irreducible *svarūpa-bheda*: not just numerical distinction but qualitative difference belonging to its own form. This *svarūpa-bheda* grounds the doctrine of *taratamya* and forecloses any reading on which release dissolves the *jīva* into Hari. Hari himself has a *svarūpa* in which *guṇa* and *guṇin* are non-different yet really distinguishable through *viśeṣa*.

Bhagavān's *svarūpa-śakti* is the internal energy that constitutes his own essential being and his eternal *līlā*; this is distinguished from *taṭasthā-śakti* (the *jīvas*) and *bahiraṅgā-śakti* (*mahāmāyā*). The *jīva*'s *svarūpa* is *cid-aṃśa* of Bhagavān, eternal servant in a specific *rasa*. Bondage masks but does not destroy the *svarūpa*; *sādhana* uncovers it.

For Bhāskara, *svarūpa-bheda* between Brahman and *jīva* would compromise the *abheda*-śruti; difference is therefore *aupādhika*, real but conditional. Nimbārka's *svābhāvika-bhedābheda* moves the other way: difference belongs to the *svarūpa* itself, simultaneously with non-difference. The dispute is precisely about whether *svarūpa* can sustain both *bheda* and *abheda* together.

*Svarūpa-sambandha* is one of the standard relational categories: a relation that holds by the very nature of the relata rather than through a third entity (as *samavāya* would). It does work in accounts of *abhāva*'s relation to its locus and in puzzles about the relation of property and property-bearer.

translator_note: 'Essence' captures only part. *Svarūpa* is not Aristotelian *to ti ēn einai*, and not a bare property cluster; it is the form a thing has as itself, often paired explicitly with *taṭastha* (extrinsic-mark) definition. Where the school is making a point about intrinsic versus conditioned difference, leave the term untranslated.
